title: Angled Adirondack Footstool
summary: A low, raked-leg footstool with a decked 1x4 top on two 30°-angled stringers — built for reclining with an Adirondack chair, distinct from this catalog's boxier Adirondack side table.
description:
This footstool's legs are cut at a genuine 30° rake rather than standing square, matching the reclined angle of an Adirondack chair rather than sitting bolt upright the way a side table would — a deliberately different build from this catalog's flat-legged Adirondack side table. Trim the bottom ends of the stringers to their angle first, since that's what sets the whole stool's lean.

Deck the top with the 1x4 boards, spaced evenly and fastened with nails or screws, then attach the angled legs last and flip the whole thing over. **This is a genuinely low, three-piece-per-side build** — no box frame, no apron, just stringers, decking, and legs — which is what makes it the quicker of the two small Adirondack companion pieces in this catalog.

materials.name: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 30° off square, parallel to each other, long point to short point measurement.
One end cut at 30° off square, longest point measurement.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Set the miter saw to 30° and cut every angled part before you touch the setting. From the 2x4: two stringers at 23-1/2" with both ends at 30° and parallel to each other (long point to short point), and two legs at 13-3/4" with one end at 30°, measured to the long point. Both stringers and both legs come out of one 8 ft 2x4 — 74-1/2" of a 96" board. From the 1x4, cut five top boards at 22-1/2"; the 12 ft board holds all five at 112-1/2" and leaves a genuinely useful offcut.

Hold the two stringers face to face before going any further and confirm the angles match exactly. That 30° is what tilts the top to meet your legs when you are reclined in the chair, and two stringers that disagree give you a top that twists instead of a stool that fits.

steps.title: Set the stringers up
steps.body:
Stand the two 23-1/2" stringers on edge and parallel, with both 30° slopes running the same way — that shared slope is the seat tilt, so a stringer flipped end for end will fight the other one and the top will never sit true.

The 22-1/2" top boards run across them, so set the spacing between the stringers such that the boards overhang each one by the same amount. Measure the gap between the stringers at the front and at the back and make those two measurements agree before you fasten anything; the plan gives no spacing dimension, so parallel-and-even is the standard you work to.

steps.title: Add the decking
steps.body:
Lay the five 22-1/2" 1x4 top boards across the stringers with a small even gap between them — about 1/4" so rain drains rather than sits — and their ends flush with the outside faces of the stringers. Predrill near every board end so the 3/4" stock does not split, then drive two 2" exterior self-tapping screws through each board into each stringer.

Five boards at 3-1/2" plus four 1/4" gaps covers 18-1/2" of the 23-1/2" stringers, so decide where that block of decking sits along the stringer before the first screw goes in. Use a 1/4" offcut as a spacer at every gap rather than measuring each one.

steps.title: Add the legs and flip
steps.body:
Flip the assembly decking-down. Set a 13-3/4" leg under each end of the stringers with its 30° cut lying flat on the floor and its square end up against the stringer, so the sloped top ends up level side to side and the stool stands solid front to back. Fasten each leg with two or three 2-1/2" exterior screws driven through the stringer into the leg.

Turn it right side up on a flat floor and check that all four contact points touch. If it rocks, the two legs are not registered the same way against the stringers — fix that now rather than shimming it later.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body:
Sand the board tops and ease every edge and corner; bare feet and calves land on this, and cedar or pine splinters find them. Coat everything with exterior penetrating stain or paint, including the end grain and the bottoms of the legs, which sit on wet decking or grass.

Set it in front of your Adirondack chair and check that the slope meets your outstretched legs — that fit is the entire reason for the 30° cuts, and it is the only test that matters.
materials: Exterior penetrating wood stain or paint

images.alt: Angled footstool for an Adirondack chair
